expanded_description: A comprehensive capstone review of chemical nomenclature, integrating naming rules for ionic, covalent, acid, and hydrate compounds into a unified decision-making framework, with extensive practice to master translating between names and formulas.

core_concept.principle: Successful nomenclature requires first identifying the compound type (ionic, covalent, acid, or hydrate) before applying specific naming rules; systematic identification prevents errors and ensures accurate communication.
core_concept.explanation: Chemical nomenclature is not a single set of rules but a decision tree. The first step is always classification: Does the formula start with H? (Acid). Does it contain a metal? (Ionic). Does it contain only nonmetals? (Covalent). Does it have a dot and water? (Hydrate). Once classified, specific rules apply. Mastery comes from practicing this identification process repeatedly until it becomes automatic.

image.alt: Flowchart showing decision tree for identifying compound type and naming rules
image.caption: Nomenclature Decision Tree: Identify compound type first, then apply specific naming rules.

sections.introduction: Before naming any compound or writing any formula, you must identify what type of compound it is. This decision tree guides you through the classification process step-by-step.

key_concept.definition: The nomenclature decision tree is a systematic flowchart: (1) Is it a hydrate? (look for $\cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O}$), (2) Is it an acid? (starts with H), (3) Is it ionic? (contains metal or $\text{NH}_4^+$), (4) Is it covalent? (only nonmetals).
key_concept.context: This order matters because hydrates and acids have special rules that override general ionic/covalent rules. Always check for these first.

principles.statement: If the formula contains a dot followed by water ($\cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O}$), it is a hydrate; name the ionic/covalent part first, then add Greek prefix + 'hydrate'.
principles.application: $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$: Identify $\text{CuSO}_4$ (ionic) first, then add 'pentahydrate'. Ignore water until the end.
principles.example: $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \cdot 10\text{H}_2\text{O}$: Sodium carbonate (ionic part) + decahydrate. Always separate water naming from main compound.
principles.analogy: Like addressing an envelope: write the address (compound name) first, then add 'Care Of' (hydrate) at the end.

principles.statement: If the formula starts with H (and is aqueous), it is an acid; use 'hydro-' for binary acids or '-ic/-ous' for oxyacids.
principles.application: $\text{HCl}$: Starts with H, no O → hydrochloric acid. $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$: Starts with H, has O → sulfuric acid (from sulfate).
principles.example: $\text{HNO}_3$: Starts with H, has O → nitric acid (from nitrate). Never use 'hydro-' for oxyacids.
principles.analogy: Like a VIP badge: 'H' at the start signals special acid rules apply.

principles.statement: If the compound contains a metal (or $\text{NH}_4^+$) and nonmetal(s), it is ionic; use cation name + anion name, with Roman numerals for variable-charge metals.
principles.application: $\text{FeCl}_3$: Fe is metal → ionic. Fe is variable → iron(III). Cl is chloride → iron(III) chloride.
principles.example: $\text{Na}_2\text{O}$: Na is metal (fixed) → sodium. O is oxide → sodium oxide. No Roman numeral needed for Group 1.
principles.analogy: Like a handshake: metal gives electrons, nonmetal takes them; name reflects the partners.

principles.statement: If the compound contains only nonmetals (no H at start, no metal), it is covalent; use Greek prefixes for both elements.
principles.application: $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4$: Only nonmetals → covalent. di- + tetra- → dinitrogen tetroxide.
principles.example: $\text{CO}_2$: Only nonmetals → covalent. Carbon (no mono-) + dioxide → carbon dioxide.
principles.analogy: Like sharing a meal: both sides contribute (share electrons), prefixes show how much each brings.

worked_examples.title: Classifying Compounds Using the Decision Tree
worked_examples.problem: Identify the compound type for: (a) $\text{P}_4\text{O}_{10}$, (b) $\text{Mg}(\text{NO}_3)_2 \cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$, (c) $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$, (d) $\text{Cu}_2\text{S}$. Explain your reasoning.

step_by_step.calculation: Hydrate? No dot. Acid? Doesn't start with H. Ionic? No metal (P and O are nonmetals). → Covalent.
Hydrate? Yes, has $\cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$. → Hydrate (specifically, ionic hydrate).
Hydrate? No. Acid? Starts with H. → Acid (specifically, oxyacid).
Hydrate? No. Acid? No. Ionic? Yes, Cu is metal. → Ionic (specifically, Type II ionic).

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Covalent (only nonmetals). (b) Hydrate (has water dot). (c) Acid (starts with H). (d) Ionic (contains metal). Correct classification is the critical first step before applying naming rules.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using the nomenclature decision tree to classify compounds before naming

sections.introduction: Once you've identified the compound type, apply the specific naming rules for that category. This summary table consolidates all rules from Unit 5 for quick reference.

key_concept.definition: Naming rules summary: Ionic (cation + anion, Roman numerals if Type II), Covalent (prefixes for both), Acid (hydro- for binary, -ic/-ous for oxy), Hydrate (ionic/covalent name + prefix + hydrate).
key_concept.context: Keep this summary handy during practice. Over time, you'll memorize these patterns, but initially, refer to this guide to ensure accuracy.

classifications.behavior: Name metal unchanged. Name nonmetal with -ide. Name polyatomic from memory.

examples.explanation: K (Group 1) + O. Name: Potassium oxide. Formula: $2\text{K}^+ + \text{O}^{2-} \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{O}$.
Ca (Group 2) + $\text{OH}^-$. Name: Calcium hydroxide. Formula: Parentheses needed for 2 $\text{OH}^-$.

classifications.behavior: Calculate metal charge from anion charge. Include Roman numeral in name. Balance for formula.

examples.explanation: Fe + $3\text{Cl}^-$. Fe must be $+3$. Name: Iron(III) chloride. Formula: $\text{Fe}^{3+} + 3\text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{FeCl}_3$.
Cu + $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$. Cu must be $+2$. Name: Copper(II) sulfate. Formula: $\text{Cu}^{2+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{CuSO}_4$.

classifications.behavior: Translate prefixes to subscripts for formulas. Add prefixes to names based on subscripts.

examples.explanation: 2 N (di-) + 5 O (penta-). Name: Dinitrogen pentoxide. Formula: Prefixes directly give subscripts.
1 P (no prefix) + 3 Cl (tri-). Name: Phosphorus trichloride. Formula: Subscripts directly give prefixes.

classifications.behavior: Identify anion. Apply acid suffix rule. Add 'acid' at end. Formula: $\text{H}^+$ + anion.

examples.explanation: Anion: Chloride (binary). Name: Hydrochloric acid. Formula: $\text{H}^+ + \text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{HCl}$.
Anion: Sulfite (-ite). Name: Sulfurous acid. Formula: $2\text{H}^+ + \text{SO}_3^{2-} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_3$.

classifications.behavior: Name ionic/covalent part normally. Add prefix for water count. Formula: Compound $\cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O}$.

examples.explanation: Ionic: Magnesium sulfate. Water: 7 (hepta-). Name: Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate.

components.definition: Quick reference for common polyatomic ions
components.interpretation: Memorize these polyatomic ions; they appear frequently in ionic compounds, acids, and hydrates. Knowing them speeds up nomenclature significantly.

sections.section_title: Mixed Practice: Naming and Formula Writing
sections.introduction: Now apply all rules together. These practice problems mix all compound types to test your ability to classify first, then name or write formulas accurately.

key_concept.definition: Mixed practice requires switching between naming rules fluidly; always classify the compound type before attempting to name or write the formula.
key_concept.context: This is the ultimate test of Unit 5 mastery. If you can accurately handle these mixed problems, you are ready for Unit 6 (Chemical Reactions).

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Nomenclature Practice Set
worked_examples.problem: Name or write the formula for each: (a) $\text{Li}_2\text{CO}_3$, (b) dinitrogen tetroxide, (c) $\text{Fe}(\text{NO}_3)_3$, (d) hydrofluoric acid, (e) $\text{Na}_2\text{S} \cdot 9\text{H}_2\text{O}$, (f) $\text{PbO}_2$, (g) acetic acid, (h) $\text{Cl}_2\text{O}_7$.

step_by_step.calculation: Type: Ionic (Li metal). Li is Group 1 (fixed). $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ = carbonate. Name: Lithium carbonate.
Type: Covalent (prefixes). di- = 2 N, tetra- = 4 O. Formula: $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4$.
Type: Ionic (Fe metal). Fe is variable. $3\text{NO}_3^-$ = $-3$, so Fe = $+3$. Name: Iron(III) nitrate.
Type: Acid (hydro- = binary). Fluorine → fluoride. Formula: $\text{H}^+ + \text{F}^- \rightarrow \text{HF}$.
Type: Hydrate. Ionic: $\text{Na}_2\text{S}$ = sodium sulfide. Water: 9 = nona-. Name: Sodium sulfide nonahydrate.
Type: Ionic (Pb metal). Pb is variable. $2\text{O}^{2-}$ = $-4$, so Pb = $+4$. Name: Lead(IV) oxide.
Type: Acid (common name). Systematic: ethanoic acid. Formula: $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ or $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2$.
Type: Covalent (only nonmetals). 2 Cl = di-, 7 O = hepta-. Name: Dichlorine heptoxide.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Lithium carbonate. (b) $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4$. (c) Iron(III) nitrate. (d) $\text{HF}$. (e) Sodium sulfide nonahydrate. (f) Lead(IV) oxide. (g) $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$. (h) Dichlorine heptoxide. Success requires correct classification before applying rules.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying mixed nomenclature rules across all compound types

worked_examples.title: Advanced Challenge: Complex Compounds
worked_examples.problem: Name or write formulas for these challenging compounds: (a) $\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2$, (b) ammonium dichromate, (c) $\text{HClO}_2$, (d) copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate, (e) $\text{P}_4\text{S}_3$.

step_by_step.calculation: Type: Ionic (Hg metal). Hg is variable. $2\text{Cl}^-$ = $-2$, so $\text{Hg}_2$ = $+2$, each Hg = $+1$. Name: Mercury(I) chloride.
Type: Ionic (polyatomic cation + anion). $\text{NH}_4^+$ + $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-}$. Need 2 $\text{NH}_4^+$. Formula: $(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$.
Type: Acid (starts with H, has O). Anion: $\text{ClO}_2^-$ = chlorite. -ite → -ous. Name: Chlorous acid.
Type: Hydrate. Ionic: Copper(II) = $\text{Cu}^{2+}$, Sulfate = $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ → $\text{CuSO}_4$. Water: penta- = 5. Formula: $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
Type: Covalent (only nonmetals). 4 P = tetra-, 3 S = tri-. Name: Tetraphosphorus trisulfide.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Mercury(I) chloride. (b) $(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$. (c) Chlorous acid. (d) $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$. (e) Tetraphosphorus trisulfide. These problems test edge cases (polyatomic ions, hydrates, variable charges, covalent prefixes).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Solving advanced nomenclature problems with multiple rule layers

sections.introduction: Even experienced chemists make nomenclature errors. Review these common pitfalls to ensure your work is accurate before submitting assignments or labeling chemicals.

key_concept.definition: Common nomenclature errors include: forgetting Roman numerals for Type II metals, using prefixes for ionic compounds, mixing up -ic/-ous acid suffixes, and omitting parentheses for polyatomic ions.
key_concept.context: Use this checklist before finalizing any name or formula. Catching these errors early prevents confusion in later units (especially stoichiometry and reactions).

principles.statement: Always ask: Is the metal Type II (variable charge)? If yes, Roman numeral is REQUIRED. If no (Group 1, 2, Al, Zn, Ag), NO Roman numeral.
principles.application: $\text{NaCl}$: Na is Group 1 → No Roman numeral. $\text{FeCl}_3$: Fe is transition → Iron(III) chloride (REQUIRED).
principles.example: Error: 'Copper sulfate'. Correction: 'Copper(II) sulfate' (Cu is variable). Error: 'Sodium(I) chloride'. Correction: 'Sodium chloride' (Na is fixed).
principles.analogy: Like an ID badge: Variable-charge metals need extra ID (Roman numeral) to show which version they are.

principles.statement: Prefixes (mono-, di-, tri-) are ONLY for covalent compounds. NEVER use prefixes for ionic compounds.
principles.application: $\text{CO}_2$: Covalent → Carbon dioxide (prefix OK). $\text{Na}_2\text{O}$: Ionic → Sodium oxide (NO 'disodium').
principles.example: Error: 'Disodium oxide'. Correction: 'Sodium oxide' (ionic charges determine ratio, not prefixes). Error: 'Nitrogen oxide'. Correction: 'Nitrogen monoxide' or 'Nitrogen dioxide' (must specify).
principles.analogy: Like currency: Ionic uses charge 'coins' to balance; Covalent uses prefix 'counters' to specify.

principles.statement: Remember: '-ate' goes with '-ic', '-ite' goes with '-ous'. 'Hydro-' ONLY for binary acids (no oxygen).
principles.application: Nitrate ($\text{NO}_3^-$) → Nitric acid. Nitrite ($\text{NO}_2^-$) → Nitrous acid. Chloride ($\text{Cl}^-$) → Hydrochloric acid.
principles.example: Error: 'Hydronitric acid'. Correction: 'Nitric acid' (has oxygen, no 'hydro-'). Error: 'Sulfate acid'. Correction: 'Sulfuric acid' (-ate → -ic).
principles.analogy: Like a matching game: -ate/-ic are both 'fat' words (more letters), -ite/-ous are both 'thin' words (fewer letters).

principles.statement: Use parentheses around polyatomic ions in formulas ONLY when subscript > 1. Never use parentheses for monatomic ions.
principles.application: $\text{Mg}(\text{NO}_3)_2$: Parentheses needed (2 nitrates). $\text{MgCl}_2$: No parentheses (Cl is monatomic).
principles.example: Error: '$\text{MgNO}_3{}_2$'. Correction: '$\text{Mg}(\text{NO}_3)_2$' (shows 2 complete nitrate ions). Error: '$\text{Na}(\text{Cl})_2$'. Correction: '$\text{NaCl}_2$' (but actually NaCl, Na is +1).
principles.analogy: Like a bundle: Parentheses wrap the bundle (polyatomic) when you need more than one bundle.

worked_examples.title: Error Correction Practice
worked_examples.problem: Identify the error in each and provide the correct name/formula: (a) 'Copper chloride' for $\text{CuCl}_2$, (b) '$\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$' named 'disodium carbonate', (c) 'Hydrosulfuric acid' for $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, (d) '$\text{Fe}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$' written as '$\text{Fe}_2\text{SO}_4{}_3$'.

step_by_step.calculation: Error: Missing Roman numeral. Cu is variable. $2\text{Cl}^-$ means Cu is $+2$. Correct: Copper(II) chloride.
Error: Prefixes for ionic compound. Na is Group 1. Correct: Sodium carbonate (no 'di-').
Error: 'Hydro-' for oxyacid. $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ has oxygen. Anion = sulfate. Correct: Sulfuric acid.
Error: Missing parentheses for polyatomic. Correct: $\text{Fe}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$ (shows 3 complete sulfate ions).

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Copper(II) chloride (add Roman numeral). (b) Sodium carbonate (remove prefixes). (c) Sulfuric acid (remove 'hydro-', use -ic). (d) $\text{Fe}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$ (add parentheses). Reviewing errors reinforces correct rules.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Identifying and correcting common nomenclature errors

summary: Unit 5 Nomenclature Mastery: Successfully naming compounds requires a systematic approach: (1) Classify type (Hydrate → Acid → Ionic → Covalent), (2) Apply specific rules (Ionic: charges + Roman numerals if Type II; Covalent: prefixes; Acid: hydro-/-ic/-ous; Hydrate: + prefix + hydrate), (3) Verify (check charges, prefixes, parentheses). Key distinctions: Ionic uses charges (no prefixes), Covalent uses prefixes (no charges), Acids start with H, Hydrates have water dot. Common errors to avoid: missing Roman numerals for variable metals, using prefixes for ionic compounds, mixing acid suffixes, omitting parentheses for polyatomics. Practice mixed problems to build fluency. Mastery of nomenclature is essential for Unit 6 (Chemical Reactions) where you will write and balance chemical equations using these names and formulas.
